title: Matrescence: The Identity Rewrite
subtitle: A reading on the psychological transition of becoming a mother and why it rarely gets named
excerpt: A reading on matrescence, the developmental transition of becoming a mother. Looks at what changes in a woman's identity when she becomes a mother, why that change is rarely acknowledged, and what it means to go through a major identity shift without a cultural script for it.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on the work of Dana Raphael (who coined 'matrescence' in 1973) and later research by anthropologist and academic Aurelie Athan, as well as Jess Klingbaum's contemporary writing on the subject.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you feel like you have lost yourself in motherhood, or when the transition to becoming a mother feels more like a loss than a gain and you want a framework for what is happening.

content.text: A reading on matrescence, the psychological and developmental transition of becoming a mother. The word was coined by anthropologist Dana Raphael in 1973 and has been largely absent from mainstream discourse since. What it names is the identity shift of becoming a mother: a transition as significant as adolescence, with no cultural script to accompany it.

Matrescence is the process of becoming a mother. It is not the birth, not the postnatal period, and not a single event. It is a developmental transition that involves changes to identity, values, priorities, relationships, body, and sense of self. It is, in its scope, comparable to adolescence.

Adolescence gets a cultural framework. There are rites of passage for it, literature about it, social recognition of it as a period of significant change. Matrescence has almost none of that. Women are expected to manage the transition while simultaneously being fully functional mothers, partners, and workers. The confusion, loss, and grief that many women feel during the transition are frequently framed as mental health problems rather than as the predictable features of a major developmental shift.

The identity changes in matrescence are not just about adding the role of mother. The woman who becomes a mother is a different person from the woman who existed before. Neuroscience research has found structural brain changes associated with pregnancy and new motherhood. The neurological and hormonal changes are real and significant.

At the level of identity, the changes include: a restructuring of priorities; a shift in how time is experienced; changes to friendships and what they are for; a different relationship with the body; and often a reassessment of values that were previously taken for granted. None of this is abnormal. All of it takes time to integrate.

Many women grieve who they were before they became mothers. They grieve the freedom, the spontaneity, the version of themselves that existed without this level of responsibility. That grief is real and it is not pathological. It does not mean they regret their children or that they are failing at motherhood. It means they are aware of what was given up, and giving up something real is worth mourning.

The cultural insistence that motherhood is entirely joyful makes this grief very hard to speak. Women who admit to mourning their pre-mother selves are often told, directly or indirectly, that this means they don't love their children enough. It doesn't. It means they are giving an accurate account of what the transition cost.

Having a word for matrescence, and a framework for understanding it as a developmental transition rather than a crisis or a failure, changes the experience. Women who understand what is happening to them during the transition report being better able to tolerate it, more patient with the confusion, and less likely to pathologise normal responses to an abnormally demanding change.

The transition does not have a fixed endpoint. Some women feel it is largely complete within a few years. Others find the identity continues to shift as their children grow and their role changes with them. The lack of a fixed endpoint is not a failure of integration. It is the nature of a living identity.

Written for homemade.education. The concept of matrescence was introduced by anthropologist Dana Raphael in 1973 and developed more recently by psychologist and academic Aurelie Athan, whose research has been central to bringing the concept into contemporary maternal health discussions.
